verses.commentary: On the first day of the week, at early dawn (orthrou bathēos, ὄρθρου βαθέως), the women went to the tomb, taking the spices they had prepared. The first resurrection witnesses are women — culturally invalid witnesses (Josephus). God chose so.
Found the stone rolled away. The first sign — physical access already arranged.
Entering, did not find the body of the Lord Jesus. Absence is the evidence.
While perplexed (aporeisthai, ἀπορεῖσθαι) — two men in dazzling apparel stood by them. Angelic witnesses appear at moments of comprehension-failure.
Terrified, faces bowed to the ground. 'Why do you seek the living among the dead?' Theological question reorients the search.
'He is not here, but is risen. Remember how he told you while still in Galilee.' Memory invoked — they had been told this would happen.
Said: 'The Son of Man must be delivered into the hands of sinful men, be crucified, and on the third day rise.' Passion predictions cited verbatim.
They remembered his words. Memory works backward as well as forward.
Returning from the tomb, told all these things to the Eleven and all the rest. The women become apostles to the apostles.
Mary Magdalene, Joanna (wife of Herod's manager — Luke 8:3), Mary the mother of James, and the other women told these things to the apostles. Named witnesses, identifiable in the community.
Their words seemed to them an idle tale (leros, λῆρος — nonsense), and they did not believe them. The apostles' first response to resurrection news: dismissal.
Peter rose, ran to the tomb. Stooping, saw only the linen cloths. Went home marveling at what had happened. Investigation, not yet faith.
That very day, two of them were going to a village named Emmaus, about seven miles from Jerusalem. Lukan unique — the road to Emmaus account begins.
Talking with each other about all these things that had happened. Processing aloud.
While they were talking and discussing — Jesus himself drew near and went with them. The risen Lord travels incognito.
Their eyes were kept (ekratounto, ἐκρατοῦντο — held) from recognizing him. Divine concealment by design — the recognition will come later, by his agency.
'What is this discussion (logoi, λόγοι) you are exchanging?' They stood still, looking sad. Faces tell the story.
One named Cleopas: 'Are you the only visitor to Jerusalem who does not know the things that have happened?' Ironic — the only one in town who fully knows.

'What things?' 'Concerning Jesus of Nazareth, a man who was a prophet mighty in deed and word before God and all the people.' Their Christology: prophet, mighty in word and deed. Inadequate but honest.
How our chief priests and rulers delivered him up to be condemned to death and crucified him.
'We had hoped (elpizomen, ἠλπίζομεν) he was the one to redeem Israel.' Past tense — hope frustrated. 'Besides — this is the third day.' Three-day-marker noticed but not yet connected to prediction.
'Some women of our company amazed us — early at the tomb,'
'did not find his body, came saying they had seen a vision of angels, who said he was alive.' Women's testimony repeated but not believed.
'Some of those with us went to the tomb — found it as the women had said — but him they did not see.' Peter's tomb-visit confirmed absence, not resurrection.
'O foolish (anoetoi, ἀνόητοι) ones, slow of heart to believe all that the prophets have spoken!' Diagnosis is hermeneutical: they have not read scripture christologically.
'Was it not necessary (edei, ἔδει) that the Christ should suffer these things and enter into his glory?' Suffering BEFORE glory is the necessary sequence.

Beginning with Moses and all the Prophets — interpreted to them in all the Scriptures the things concerning himself. Christological exposition of OT. The hermeneutic of Christ is taught by Christ himself.
Drew near the village — he acted as if he would go further. Politeness — does not impose.
They urged him strongly: 'Stay with us, for it is toward evening — the day is far spent.' Hospitality offered. He went in to stay with them.
At table with them — took the bread, blessed, broke, gave to them. The fourfold action again — eucharistic shape.
Their eyes were opened, they recognized him — and he vanished (aphantos, ἄφαντος — invisible) from their sight. Recognition by table-action; departure follows recognition.

'Did not our hearts burn (kaiomene, καιομένη) within us while he talked to us on the road, while he opened (dianoigon, διανοίγων) the Scriptures to us?' Retrospective awareness. Burning hearts during scripture-opening — paradigm experience.
Same hour rose, returned to Jerusalem — found the eleven and those with them gathered together.
Saying: 'The Lord has truly risen and has appeared to Simon.' Multiple appearances corroborate. Simon (Peter) was granted private restoration.
They told what had happened on the road and how he was made known to them in the breaking of the bread. Eucharistic recognition formula.
As they were talking — Jesus himself stood among them: 'Peace be with you.' Greeting through closed doors (compare John 20:19). Resurrection-body's new mode.
Startled and frightened, they thought they saw a spirit (pneuma, πνεῦμα — ghost).
'Why are you troubled? Why do doubts (dialogismoi, διαλογισμοί) arise in your hearts?'

'See my hands and feet — that it is I myself. Handle me and see — a spirit does not have flesh and bones (sarka kai ostea, σάρκα καὶ ὀστέα) as you see I have.' Bodily resurrection emphasized against docetic readings.
Said this, showed them his hands and feet. The wounds confirm identity.
While they still disbelieved for joy and were marveling — 'Have you anything here to eat?' Joy combined with disbelief. Lukan psychologically realistic detail.
Gave him a piece of broiled fish.
Took it and ate it before them. Eating ratifies bodily reality — ghosts do not eat fish.

'These are the words I spoke while still with you — that everything written about me in the Law of Moses, the Prophets, and the Psalms must be fulfilled.' Tripartite Hebrew canon (Torah, Nevi'im, Ketuvim) — comprehensive scriptural witness to Christ.
Then he opened their minds (dienoixen ton noun, διήνοιξεν τὸν νοῦν) to understand the Scriptures. Comprehension is gift, not just effort. Same word as Emmaus 'opened.'
'Thus it is written: the Christ should suffer, rise from the dead the third day.' Three-day-resurrection-pattern named as fulfilled Scripture.

'Repentance and forgiveness of sins should be proclaimed in his name to all nations (panta ta ethne, πάντα τὰ ἔθνη) — beginning from Jerusalem.' The Gentile mission inaugurated. Jerusalem becomes departure point for global mission. Acts setup.
'You are witnesses (martyres, μάρτυρες) of these things.' Eyewitness role assigned.
'Behold, I send the promise of my Father upon you — stay in the city until you are clothed with power from on high.' Spirit-promise (Joel 2:28-29; Acts 2). Power-from-on-high mission-empowerment.
Led them out as far as Bethany — lifted up his hands, blessed them. Priestly posture (Leviticus 9:22). The Messiah blesses as his final earthly act.
While he was blessing them — he parted from them, was carried up (anephereto, ἀνεφέρετο) into heaven. Ascension during blessing — the blessing continues unfinished.
They worshiped him, returned to Jerusalem with great joy. Worship of Jesus by his disciples — high Christology in the resurrection community.

Continually in the temple, blessing God. Lukan symmetry — Gospel began in the temple (Zechariah, 1:8-10) and ends in the temple (apostles, 24:53). Praise frames the Gospel. The next volume (Acts) will take this praise to the ends of the earth.
